NAV/COM Systems.clue: Glideslope, localizer, VOR, GPS, and ILS receivers are grouped together as components of this onboard functional category.
NAV/COM Systems.explanation: All five systems provide position or course guidance, making them navigation systems. Communications, weather, and flight control are separate categories with distinct purposes.

NAV/COM Systems.clue: For aeronautical voice traffic, VHF airborne transceivers are tuned within this dedicated segment of the spectrum.
NAV/COM Systems.explanation: VHF aviation COM is allocated 118.000 to 136.975 MHz. The 108-117.95 MHz band is VOR/LOC, 2-30 MHz is HF, and 329-335 MHz is the glideslope band.

NAV/COM Systems.clue: This is the allocated frequency band for VOR and localizer receivers on civil aircraft.
NAV/COM Systems.explanation: VOR and LOC share 108.0 to 117.95 MHz, with odd decimals reserved for LOC. COM is 118.0+ MHz, marker beacons operate on 75 MHz, and glideslope uses 329-335 MHz.

NAV/COM Systems.clue: Every marker beacon ground transmitter in the national airspace broadcasts on this single fixed frequency.
NAV/COM Systems.explanation: Outer, middle, and inner markers all broadcast on 75 MHz and are distinguished only by their modulation tones. 121.5 is emergency voice, 108 MHz is nav, and 1030 MHz is transponder interrogation.

NAV/COM Systems.clue: This is what the pilot accomplishes by briefly keying an HF transmitter after selecting a new frequency.
NAV/COM Systems.explanation: The initial key-up drives the automatic HF antenna coupler through its tuning cycle to minimize SWR. None of the other options are actions that PTT initiates.

NAV/COM Systems.clue: This piece of test equipment measures forward and reflected power to yield the standing wave ratio of a COM antenna.
NAV/COM Systems.explanation: An inline directional wattmeter measures forward and reflected power so SWR can be calculated. Signal generators create RF for receiver tests, scopes view waveforms, and transponder sets test replies.

NAV/COM Systems.clue: When a COM radio transmits normally but produces no receive audio even at full volume, this is the first setting to check.
choices: Squelch may be set too high
PTT switch may be stuck on
NAV/COM Systems.explanation: An over-tight squelch silences normal receive. High SWR would also affect transmit, a weak final would degrade transmit, and a stuck PTT would lock the radio in transmit.

NAV/COM Systems.clue: When a VOR shows this flag while the aircraft is within published station range, the signal has dropped below the usable threshold.
choices: A message that no station is tuned
An autopilot that is not coupled
An OBS set to the wrong radial
A NAV flag indicating unusable signal
NAV/COM Systems.explanation: The NAV flag indicates the received signal is below the usable threshold. A missing station tuning would not show a flag, OBS setting does not trigger it, and autopilot coupling is a separate issue.

NAV/COM Systems.clue: False glideslope captures on ILS approaches come from this part of the transmitted signal.
choices: Side lobes above the correct path
The main beam at the correct angle
NAV/COM Systems.explanation: Side lobes above the main glideslope beam produce false on-path indications. The main beam is the legitimate path, back course is a localizer phenomenon, and marker coverage is unrelated.

NAV/COM Systems.clue: DME computes slant range using this timing measurement between the airborne interrogator and the ground transponder.
NAV/COM Systems.explanation: DME times the round-trip delay of its interrogation and the transponder reply to compute slant range. Amplitude, Doppler, and phase methods are used by other systems.
